title.string: Web Development - Build a Web Page

description.string: Using the essential XHTML elements to build and display a web page.

item.value: To use the XHTML table elements correctly on a web page.

para.b: pic001.jpgBuild a Web Page - Using the secret codes of the Web.

newpage.para: This tutorial will show you how to build and view a web page using a simple text editor and a browser.
You will learn some of the secret codes that browsers use to display the text on a page as well as how a web page is organized.

para.b: You need two tools to create web pages:
A color-coded Text Editor

newpage.para: Notepad++http://portableapps.com/apps/development/notepadpp_portable (Windows) - You can run this from your flash drive.
TextWranglerhttp://www.barebones.com/products/textwrangler/ (Macintosh)

para.b: A Browser

newpage.para: - FireFoxhttp://www.ie7.com/ is recommended the highest. FireFox is very compatible with current standards and has many great development tools.
- Google Chromehttp://www.google.com/chrome is also good and has many tools built in.
- Microsoft Internet Explorer (IE) is worst. It is not compatible with current standards.
Run both programs (the text editor and the browser) side-by-side so you can easily switch back and forth between the two.
Windows Tip: Right-mouse click on the Start bar and choose the horizontal display option.
Don't use a word processor like Word or WordPad. They add in hidden characters that will show up as weird text in the browser.
Cache (pronounced "cash") is a holding area in the computer's memory. The browser displays the web page located in cache instead of going back out to the web server that takes more time.

pic003.jpgFor a developer this is not a good thing because the browser will keep looking at the old version of a page in the cache instead of going to the file system and getting the most recent copy with your changes.
To fix this problem set the cache to zero on all the browsers you use.
In FireFox Tools/Options/Advanced/Network tab
Use up to 0 MB of space for the cache.
Click on the "Clear Now" button to empty the current cache.
Short cut to clear the cache: Tools/Clear Recent History/Details uncheck everything except "Cache"
In Internet Explorer (IE) - Tools/Internet Options/General Tab
In the "Browsing history" area click on the "Settings" button
Select the option: "Every time I visit the webpage"
Set the "Disk space to use" to 8.

para.b: Here are the essential XHTML elements.

newpage.para: Notice that the markup tags come in pairs.
<html> and </html>
<body> and </body>
and
The first tag is called the start tag: <body>
The second tag is called the stop tag: </body>

para.b: Type this text into your editor.

newpage.para: Name the file: test.html
<html>
<head>
<title>This is a test page</title>
</head>
<body>
Test Page
This is a test paragraph.
<.body.
</html>

para.b: To view the file in the browser use:

newpage.para: File/Open File/ locate the file test.html
Notice that you don't have to be connected to the Web. The browser is simply translating your text using the markup tags.
Here is a screen shot showing how the various "secret" codes appear in the browser:
The <title> element displays in the bar along the top of the browser as well as in the tab for that page. In Windows the <title> information also displays along the bottom in the Start menu bar.
Important: All of the information that appears on the browser page must be inside the <body> and </body> tags.
The <h1> is a header and displays as a heading in the page itself.

The text inside of the

element appears as a paragraph. The words will automatically wrap if the user changes the size of the browser window.

para.b: The browser is always looking for the Less Than character "<".

newpage.para: When it sees one it knows that is the start of a markup command. After the < it reads each character until it gets to the Greater Than ">" and then it looks up the command in its dictionary to see what to do.

para.b: For example:

newpage.para: tells the browser to start displaying the text that follows as a main heading. It will continue to do this until it comes to the stop h1:
If you forget to include a the entire page will be displayed as a main heading.
After the browser has read a tag it discards it. Notice that none of the tags show up on the browser page.
Elements include the start and stop tags and everything inside of them.
An element can contain other elements.

For example a

element may contain a element.
Tags are the markers at the beginning and end of each element. They tell the browser how to display a section of text.

para.b: Attributes are used to fine-tune elements.

newpage.para: For example, the element has two required attributes: src=" " and alt=" ".
Each attribute has a name, the equal sign "=" and a value inside of quotation marks.

para.b: The attributes have to be inside of the < and > of the tag.

newpage.para: Think of a tag as a sentence. Attributes are adjectives describing the subject (or tag name) of the sentence.

para.b: There are four XHTML rules:

newpage.para: (1) There can be only one root element. The <html> </html> is always the root
element of a web page.)
(2) Every start tag needs a stop tag. For example: and or for the single elements:
(3) UPPER/lower case matter. Make every tag lower case.
(4) All attribute values must be inside of quotation marks.

para.b: You can set up six different levels of headings for sub-heads, sub-sub-heads, etc.
Add this to your code:

newpage.para: A sub head
A sub-sub head
A sub-sub-sub head
Hardly a heading anymore
Save the file using CTRL s
Refresh your browser using CTRL r
Your browser should show a screen similar to this (without the green labels):

para.b: Two things the browser ignores are line-returns and more than one space.
In your code break up the lines so there is only a few words on each line.

newpage.para: Save the file (CTRL s) and view it in your browser (CTRL r).

para.b: None of the changes show up in the browser!

newpage.para: All the browsers are programmed to ignore multiple spaces and new lines. This lets the programmer format code for readability without changing the look of the finished web page.

On the next page you will see how to use the
to tell the browser to start a new line.
To add multiple spaces use the non-breaking space special character: ~

Add in some
elements in order to tell the browser to start a new line.
Remember the XHTML rules? Every start tag needs a stop tag.

The
element is a single-tag element. In order to meet the XHTML requirements we add a space slash " /" to act as a "stop" tag.

For double tag elements the slash is at the front:

and
For single tag elements the slash is tacked on at the end:

Add some
elements to the paragraph and view the results in the browser.
Notice how we used the special character ~< to display the < character. If you us the < by itself the browser thinks it is a new markup command!

para.b: If you want some text to be bold, use the <strong> element.

newpage.para: If you want to add a horizontal rule to separate areas on the page use the single-element <hr /> You can add use the width=" " attribute to set the width to be a percentage of the page.

para.b:
Type in this code as part of the

element:

newpage.para: This is a test paragraph.
With several lines.
This is more copy showing how the horizontal rule can be used to separate areas on the page.

para.b: Here is a copy of the final
file.

newpage.para: To save it to your computer right-click on the link and select "save target as" from the list of options.

para.b: You should be able to answer these questions as well as write a basic web page and display it in a browser.

list.item: What is the "magic" character that the browser uses to determine the beginning of a markup tag? What is the character that is used to determine the end of a markup tag?
What are some of the single tag elements mentioned in this tutorial?
What are the two software programs that you need to create web pages?
Do you need to be connected to the Web to write or display web pages?

para.b: This completes the tutorial: Web Development - Build a Web Page

newpage.para: July 2010
Written by Peter K. Johnson, Instructor
South Central College
North Mankato, MN 56003
